nativeRange.habitat: Indo-Pacific coral reefs and reef flats, where it lives commensally on large host sea anemones (and occasionally on corallimorphs, soft corals, jellyfish, and large sea cucumbers). Most commonly observed perched on the oral disc or tentacle bases of carpet anemones such as Stichodactyla haddoni, Stichodactyla mertensii, Heteractis crispa, Heteractis magnifica, and Entacmaea quadricolor.

decorPreferences: an established host invertebrate — ideally a large carpet anemone (Stichodactyla haddoni, S. mertensii) or other tolerant anemone (Heteractis crispa, H. magnifica, Entacmaea quadricolor)
live rock with crevices and overhangs near the chosen host
stable reef-tank lighting matched to the host anemone's requirements (carpet anemones generally need moderate-to-high reef lighting)
moderate flow consistent with a mixed reef
a tight-fitting lid (shrimp are unlikely to jump but can climb pump intakes; covers reduce evaporative loss and any rare escape)

diet.notes: In the wild it feeds primarily on mucus, food scraps, and parasites associated with its host anemone, and grazes on detritus and microorganisms drifting through the host's tentacles. In captivity it readily accepts most meaty marine foods offered to the tank — finely chopped mysis, brine shrimp, krill, marine flake, and pellet — as well as small fragments of fish or shrimp gel diets. It will often pick at the host anemone's tentacles after the anemone has been spot-fed. While it has been classified as either omnivore or carnivore depending on the source, 'omnivore' is the more representative description of its in-tank feeding behaviour.

goodWith: host sea anemones (Stichodactyla haddoni, S. mertensii, Heteractis crispa, H. magnifica, Entacmaea quadricolor)
small peaceful reef fish (gobies, blennies, firefish, cardinalfish, small wrasses)
other commensal shrimp on separate hosts (Periclimenes holthuisi, Thor amboinensis) when tank is large enough for multiple anemones
snails, peaceful hermit crabs, and other clean-up-crew invertebrates
clownfish hosted on a separate anemone of their own
avoidWith: predatory fish that target small shrimp (hawkfish, large wrasses, lionfish, triggers, puffers, large dottybacks, groupers)
anemonefish that aggressively defend the same anemone the shrimp would occupy (the shrimp is usually displaced or killed)
any copper-based medications — like all decapod crustaceans, acutely sensitive
tanks without a suitable host invertebrate — the shrimp will survive but is far more reclusive and stressed
extreme parameter swings; ensure salinity, temperature, and pH are stable before introduction

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 2677x2677. Tack-sharp side view of a single individual on its natural Mertens' carpet anemone (Stichodactyla mertensii) host; transparent body with round white spots, blue-tipped banded legs, and the diagnostic orange-and-black five-spot peacock tail fan all clearly shown. Best hero image.

Diego Delso, 2546x3819 (file title uses accepted synonym Ancylocaris brevicarpalis). Sharp dorsal/rear view showcasing the orange-black five-spot peacock tail fan, plus white body spots and blue-tipped legs. Different angle and locale from the hero shot.

Philippe Bourjon, 1200x805. Clean side profile of the whole animal on a Cryptodendrum adhaesivum host; transparent body with round white spots, black-and-white banded legs and the orange-black tail fan clearly visible. Different photographer/host for variety.

summary: The Spotted Cleaner Shrimp (Periclimenes brevicarpalis) is a small, nearly transparent commensal shrimp from the tropical Indo-Pacific, recognized by white spots on its body and distinctive orange-on-black markings on its tail fan. It is generally kept as a mated pair on a host sea anemone and is considered beginner-friendly once a suitable host is established.
careNotes:
Periclimenes brevicarpalis (Schenkel, 1902) is a small palaemonid shrimp from the tropical Indo-Pacific, ranging from Mozambique and the Red Sea east to Japan, Australia, Papua New Guinea, and Fiji. In the trade it is sold under several overlapping common names, most commonly 'Spotted Cleaner Shrimp', 'White Spot Anemone Shrimp', 'Pacific Clown Anemone Shrimp', 'Glass Anemone Shrimp', and 'Peacock-tail Anemone Shrimp'. The body is largely transparent with several round white spots irregularly placed on the carapace and abdomen, and the tail fan carries five characteristic black-rimmed orange spots that account for the 'peacock-tail' and 'five-spot' names.

Common-name disambiguation: The English name 'Spotted Cleaner Shrimp' is also applied — somewhat more correctly, in older literature — to Periclimenes yucatanicus, a separate Caribbean species that hosts on Condylactis and corkscrew anemones in the Western Atlantic. The two species look superficially similar (small, transparent, spotted, anemone-commensal) but have non-overlapping wild ranges and different host anemones. Verify the scientific name on the receipt before purchase; aquarium retailers often use the names interchangeably.

Kept in a reef aquarium, this is one of the more straightforward ornamental anemone shrimp to maintain provided a suitable host invertebrate is already established. It is almost always sold and kept as a male-female pair occupying a single host. In the wild and in tanks, P. brevicarpalis associates most often with the Haddon's carpet anemone (Stichodactyla haddoni) and Mertens' carpet (S. mertensii), but it also accepts Heteractis crispa, Heteractis magnifica, Entacmaea quadricolor (bubble-tip), and on occasion large corallimorphs, jellyfish, or sea cucumbers. The shrimp coats itself in the host's mucus to neutralise the nematocysts; after each molt it must re-approach the host to re-acquire the protective coating, so a settled host that the shrimp will revisit is important.

Water parameters are standard reef-aquarium values: temperature 22–26 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, specific gravity 1.023–1.025. The minimum practical tank size is around 115 L (30 US gallons), though the more meaningful constraint is usually the host anemone's space and lighting requirements rather than the shrimp's. In the aquarium the shrimp picks at almost anything meaty that drifts within reach, and 'omnivore' best describes its captive feeding behaviour. As with all decapod crustaceans, copper-based medications are acutely toxic and must never be used in a display housing this species.

Reef safety is well documented — the shrimp does not nip corals, will not eat live fish, and is generally ignored by most reef-safe tankmates. However, it is small and largely defenceless away from its host, so it should not be kept with predatory fish such as hawkfish, lionfish, large wrasses, triggers, puffers, or large dottybacks. Anemonefish hosting on the same anemone often displace or kill the shrimp; if both are desired, provide separate host anemones at opposite ends of the tank.

Sexual dimorphism is pronounced: females are noticeably larger than males and typically carry more conspicuous white spotting. Mated pairs are commonly observed sharing a single host in the wild, and the species is generally treated as monogamous within a host.
breedingNotes: Breeding in home aquaria is considered difficult. Hatched larvae are planktonic and pass through several zoea stages before settling — they are too small for normal reef plankton populations to support and are typically eaten or filtered out within hours. Successful rearing requires removal of larvae to a dedicated planktonkreisel (a specialized cylindrical tank designed to keep fragile planktonic larvae suspended in gentle circular flow) with appropriate microalgae and rotifer feeds, and has rarely been reported by hobbyists.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Listed as 'White Spot Anemone Shrimp' (Periclimenes brevicarpalis). Provided care level (Easy), reef-compatible (Yes), peaceful temperament, family (Palaemonidae), diet (omnivore), maximum size 2 inches, water chemistry (72-78 degF / 22.2-25.6 degC, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, specific gravity 1.023-1.025), supplement recommendations (calcium, magnesium, iodine, trace elements), host-anemone dependence, and the post-molt mucus-recoating behaviour. Common-name list ('Glass Anemone Shrimp', 'Pacific Clown Anemone Shrimp', 'White-patched Anemone Shrimp') taken from this page.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Listed as 'White Spotted Anemone Shrimp' / 'White Spot Anemone Shrimp' (Periclimenes brevicarpalis). Cross-checked: care level (Beginner — equivalent to LiveAquaria's Easy), peaceful temperament, reef compatibility (Yes), minimum tank size 30 US gallons (rounded here to 115 L), mature size 1.75 in (~4.4 cm), origin 'Indo-Pacific, Bali'. Sole source for a stated minimum tank volume. Disagrees with LiveAquaria on diet (BlueZoo: carnivore; LiveAquaria: omnivore) — omnivore taken as the better summary of captive feeding behaviour.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Periclimenes brevicarpalis
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Decapoda, Caridea, Palaemonidae, genus Periclimenes), authorship (Schenkel, 1902), Indo-Pacific distribution (tropical Pacific and Indian Oceans, including Australia), host range (sea anemones, corals, jellyfish; Stichodactyla haddoni referenced), color morphology (transparent body with white spots and orange-bordered black spots on the caudal fan), sexual dimorphism (females larger and more spotted than males), monogamous pair occupancy of a single host, and gonochoric reproductive mode. Cited body length 0.5-1.0 inch, blended with retailer maxima for the adultSizeCm range.

additional.name: Reefguide.org — Peacock-tail Anemone Shrimp (Periclimenes brevicarpalis)
additional.notes: Independent reef-photography reference confirming common-name synonymy (Peacock-tail Anemone Shrimp, Pacific Clown Anemone Shrimp, White Spot Anemone Shrimp, Glass Anemone Shrimp), Indo-Pacific distribution, and host-anemone commensalism. Used to extend the alsoKnownAs list and confirm trade-name overlap.

additional.name: Wiki.nus / NUS Taxo4254 — Periclimenes brevicarpalis (Five-spot anemone shrimp)
additional.notes: Academic species profile confirming range from Mozambique to Japan, Australia, and Papua New Guinea; depth distribution 1-15 m (some sources cite up to ~30 m); known host genera Thalassianthus, Stichodactyla, Entacmaea, Heteractis, and Parasicyonis; and the pair-on-single-host social structure. Supplied the depthRangeM (1-30 m, taken as the broader literature range) and the expanded host-anemone list.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Molts periodically throughout life as it grows. After each molt the shrimp temporarily loses the protective coating of host-anemone mucus that neutralises the host's nematocysts, and must re-approach the host to re-coat itself; during this brief window it is more vulnerable to predation and to the host's own sting. A settled, stable host anemone in the tank is therefore important. Leave shed exoskeletons in the tank — the shrimp will often re-ingest them to recover calcium and trace minerals. Iodine supplementation as part of normal reef dosing is generally considered beneficial for molting in ornamental shrimp.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Pairs spawn readily on a host anemone, but the planktonic larvae are essentially impossible to rear in a normal reef setup. Dedicated kreisel culture with appropriate microfeeds is required, and successful rearing has rarely been reported by hobbyists.
